ocr.text: The analysis of gene selection and stability is crucial for understanding the underlying biological processes and for the
development of targeted therapeutic strategies. The data presented in Table 1 highlights key metrics related to gene selection,
specifically focusing on the mean number of selected genes and the mean number of stable genes. The mean number of selected
genes is recorded at 622, indicating a robust selection process, while the mean number of stable genes stands at 504, which
constitutes approximately 80.99% of the mean number of selected genes. This high proportion underscores the reliability of the
selected genes, suggesting that a significant majority exhibit consistent expression patterns across varying conditions.
Method  Leave-seven-out Jackknife
Mean number of selected genes  Standard deviation  Mean number of stable genes and its proportion to the mean of selected genes
Furthermore, the standard deviation metric provides insight into the variability of the selected genes, although specific values
are not detailed in this context. Understanding these metrics is essential for researchers aiming to refine gene selection
methodologies, as they emphasize both the quantity and stability of genes that can potentially contribute to enhanced biological
insights and improved experimental outcomes. The implications of these findings are critical for advancing genomic research
and for the effective application of gene-targeted therapies in clinical settings.
